dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 8639 IH: Critical Operation Oversight of Law Enforcement Intervention and Training Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mrs. Sykes introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on the Judiciary

form.official-title: To develop a scenario-based training curriculum for immigration officers, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Critical Operation Oversight of Law Enforcement Intervention and Training Act or the COOL IT Act.

subsection.text: Not later than 90 days after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary of Homeland Security shall, in coordination with the Director of the Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers, develop a scenario-based training curriculum and certification process that immigration officers are required to complete annually.
In developing the curriculum under subsection (a), the Secretary of Homeland Security shall—

paragraph.text: develop a scenario-based training curriculum that addresses—

subparagraph.text: physical and emotional responses to stress;
critical decision making and problem solving;
use of force and deadly force; and

paragraph.text: consult with relevant professional law enforcement associations, community-based organizations, and defense and national security agencies in the development and dissemination of the curriculum;
provide expertise and technical assistance to components seeking to implement the curriculum;
evaluate best practices of scenario-based training methods and curriculum content to maintain state-of-the-art expertise in scenario-based learning methodology; and
develop a certification process for immigration officers that have successfully completed the curriculum.

subsection.text: Not later than 180 days after the date of enactment of this section, the Secretary of Homeland Security shall submit to Congress a report on—

paragraph.text: any benefits of, and barriers to, delivering the curriculum for immigration officers; and
recommendations for improving the access of immigration officers to scenario-based training.

subsection.text: In this section, the term immigration officer means—

paragraph.text: any employee or class of employee—

subparagraph.text: designated to perform the functions of an immigration officer within the meaning of the term under section 101 of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1101); and
designated to arrest persons under section 287(a) of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1357(a)); and

paragraph.text: any officer of a State, or any political subdivision of a State, performing functions of an immigration officer under section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1357(g)).

section.text: Section 287 of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1357) is amended by adding at the end the following:

subsection.text: Before performing duties to enforce the immigration laws, an immigration officer or employee shall be required to complete not less than 67 days of training.
